topic: Atomics - Implementing a Lock-Free Reference Counter

turns.content: Hey, I just deployed my new lock-free reference counter using AtomicUsize and it seems to work for basic counting. I'm currently doing fetch_add on clone and fetch_sub on drop with Relaxed ordering for the add operations, which feels fine to me. But when I do the decrement on drop, I'm loading the value first then subtracting one, and I want to make sure that's safe under concurrent access without holding any locks. Is there a more efficient way to handle the load-and-decrement pattern using atomics in Rust?

That makes sense for the clone path since Relaxed is fine there because you don't need to synchronize with other threads regarding the actual value being read, just that it incremented. For the drop path though, if you're doing a simple load then subtract one, you're actually exposing an ABA problem risk where another thread increments between your check and your update. If you want to avoid that race condition without locks, you should probably use compare_exchange instead of fetch_sub on the decrement side. That way you atomically try to swap the old value with new-1, and only succeed if no one else changed it in between. So your drop implementation would be something like comparing the current AtomicUsize against what you expect, then conditionally updating it only if that comparison succeeds. Also, for the load operation before decrementing, since you're checking a value to decide whether to subtract, you really need Release ordering on the store and Acquire on the load to ensure visibility across threads. If you tell me roughly how many concurrent writers you expect this counter to handle, I can suggest if Loom validation is worth it or if there's a simpler pattern.

topic: Atomics - Implementing a Lock-Free Reference Counter

turns.content: Imagine I'm building a lock-free reference counter using AtomicUsize where increment happens on clone and decrement happens on drop. If I use Relaxed ordering for the add operation but Release for the subtract, will that guarantee visibility of my changes to other threads? What about the ABA problem in this specific pattern and how does compare_exchange fit into a conditional check instead of just atomic operations?

The Relaxed order on fetch_add ensures the increment itself is visible eventually, but you must use Release on the decrement (fetch_sub) paired with Acquire when loading the counter to guarantee that any memory writes before the decrement are also made visible. The ABA problem here isn't about the value changing back to zero; it's if two threads try to decrement simultaneously and both see 1, they might both think they own a reference while another thread holds one. You can solve this with compare_exchange in your drop logic to verify ownership before allowing subtraction, or use Loom to validate the memory ordering assumptions without running actual concurrent code.

Wait, I thought Relaxed was safe for everything since it has no visibility guarantees. If my clone happens in one thread and decrement in another, how do I ensure the other thread actually sees the updated count? And if I'm trying to avoid Cell entirely, why does compare_exchange feel like I'm just copying the old value instead of truly implementing a reference counter?

Relaxed has no visibility guarantees, so you absolutely need Release on the decrement paired with Acquire when reading to ensure the update propagates correctly across threads. Regarding compare_exchange feeling like it copies values: that's exactly how a lock-free reference counter works without Cell or Mutex—the atomics provide the synchronization primitive itself. When you implement this pattern, each thread performs its own atomic operations independently, and the ordering constraints ensure consistency without shared locks. The key insight is that while compare_exchange appears to check a value, it actually acts as the visibility barrier for your decrement operation.
